title: MATH 3190 Homework 2
text:
Note that your homework should be completed in Quarto. You can just add your answers and code to this document in the appropriate part. The file should be rendered as an html document or pdf document (html is quicker to render and you will need an installation of LaTeX to render as a pdf). You will “turn in” this homework by uploading the files, the .qmd and output file, to your GitHub Math_3190_Assignment repository in the Homework/Homework_2 directory. Make sure all supporting files (if there are any) are in this directory as well. You should use git and Unix commands in a terminal for this (i.e. don’t manually upload files to GitHub). In addition, you will submit just your html or pdf file on Canvas to unlock the solutions.
Don’t forget to also put a self-critique comment on Canvas indicating what you did correctly and incorrectly and include any questions you may have about the assignment after submitting the assignment and thoroughly looking through the solutions.

title: MATH 3190 Homework 2
text:
Part e (11 points)
Many of these queries are generating outputs that are fairly small. That is, they are easily able to be stored as variables in R. A couple hundred thousand rows is fine to work with since that only takes a couple MB of RAM.

Using the dbGetQuery() function again, obtain the arrival delay times and the name of the airline (by joining with the airlines table) for all the data entries that have a delay time more than 60 minutes. Save this output as a tibble in R.


library(tibble)
delays_60=dbGetQuery(con,"SELECT flights.ARRIVAL_DELAY, airlines.AIRLINE FROM flights INNER JOIN airlines ON flights.AIRLINE = airlines.IATA_CODE WHERE flights.ARRIVAL_DELAY > 60") |> as_tibble()


Once you have this, make side-by-side boxplots in ggplot() for the arrival delay time split by the airline. This plot won’t look too great unless the y-axis is scaled. Go ahead and use the log10 scale like we did back in Lab 2. Then look up how to angle the axis labels so they do not overlap and incorporate that in your plot.


library(ggplot2)
ggplot(delays_60,aes(x=AIRLINE,y=ARRIVAL_DELAY))+geom_boxplot()+scale_y_log10()+
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, vjust = 1))+labs(x="AIRLINE", y="ARRIVAL DELAY")









Part f (10 points)

Now, let’s NOT use the dbGetQuery() function. Instead, insert an SQL code chunk for this part of the problem that selects the average arrival delay (for some reason, there is not a median function built in to SQL) and the airline’s full name for each airline. So, this output should only have 14 rows.
Then pass this output back to R and create a barplot with the airlines on the x-axis and the average arrival delay on the y-axis. Again, angle the x-axis labels so they do not overlap.


SELECT
  airlines.AIRLINE,
  AVG(flights.arrival_delay) AS average_arrival_delay
FROM flights
INNER JOIN airlines
  ON flights.AIRLINE = airlines.IATA_CODE
GROUP BY airlines.AIRLINE;


library(ggplot2)
ggplot(avg_delay_by_airline, aes(x=AIRLINE, y = average_arrival_delay)) +
  geom_col() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))+labs(x="AIRLINE",y="Average Arrival Delay")










Part g (12 points)

Now let’s use the dbplyr add-on to the dplyr package to work with an SQL database directly. You may have to install the dbplyr package. Use the tbl() function to create an object you can work with directly.


#|label: part g
library(dplyr)


Attaching package: 'dplyr'


The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':

    filter, lag


The following objects are masked from 'package:base':

    intersect, setdiff, setequal, union

library(dbplyr)


Attaching package: 'dbplyr'


The following objects are masked from 'package:dplyr':

    ident, sql

flights_tbl=tbl(con,"flights")
airlines_tbl=tbl(con,"airlines")


With that object, using R commands from the tidyverse, find the average and standard deviation of the elapsed time of the flights by airline and arrange it by the SD (with the largest at the top). Make sure to use the inner_join() function so that the name of the airline, not just its code, is given. Note that to save this object in R’s memory, you will need to do something like pipe it into the collect() function.


elapsed_time=flights_tbl |> inner_join(airlines_tbl,by=c("AIRLINE"="IATA_CODE")) |> group_by(AIRLINE) |> summarize(avg_elapsed_time=mean(ELAPSED_TIME, na.rm=T),sd_elapsed_time=sd(ELAPSED_TIME, na.rm=T)) |> arrange(desc(sd_elapsed_time)) |> collect()


Finally, make a ggplot bar graph like the one you made in Part f, but use the standard deviation as the heights on the y-axis. Why do you suppose the airline with the largest standard deviation in elapsed time has such variable flight times?


ggplot(elapsed_time,aes(x = AIRLINE, y = sd_elapsed_time))+geom_col()+theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))+labs(x = "Airline",y = "SD of Elapsed Time",)








Finally, when you are done with this assignment, it is good practice to close the connection to the flights database.

dbDisconnect(con)

title: MATH 3190 Homework 3
text:
Note that your homework should be completed in Quarto. You can just add your answers and code to this document in the appropriate part. The file should be rendered as an html document or pdf document (html is quicker to render and you will need an installation of LaTeX to render as a pdf). You will “turn in” this homework by uploading the files, the .qmd and output file, to your GitHub Math_3190_Assignment repository in the Homework/Homework_3 directory. Make sure all supporting files (if there are any) are in this directory as well. You should use git and Unix commands in a terminal for this (i.e. don’t manually upload files to GitHub). In addition, you will submit just your html or pdf file on Canvas to unlock the solutions.
Don’t forget to also put a self-critique comment on Canvas indicating what you did correctly and incorrectly and include any questions you may have about the assignment after submitting the assignment and thoroughly looking through the solutions.

Problem 1 (11 points)

Part a (9 points)
Suppose we are attempting to predict a person’s ability to run a marathon in under 4 hours (coded as a 1) based on a number of factors: age, sex, BMI, and blood pressure. Below is the confusion matrix in this situation:




Actual: 1
Actual: 0




Predicted: 1
58
102


Predicted: 0
37
217



Find each of the following. Use proper formatting in Quarto when you type your answers. You can put equations between dollar signs ($$) and you can use the \frac{}{} (for a small fraction) or \dfrac{}{} (for a larger one) commands to nicely type fractions.

The prevalence of those that can run a mile under 4 hours.
The overall accuracy of these predictions.
The sensitivity (recall).
The specificity.
The positive predictive value (precision).
The negative predictive value.
The balanced accuracy.
Cohen’s Kappa (\(\kappa\)). Check out this link on Wikipedia: Cohen’s Kappa and scroll down to the section entitled Binary classification confusion matrix.
F1 score. Check out what this is on Wikipedia: F-Score

Prevalence \[
\text{Prevalence}=\dfrac{TP+FN}{N}=\dfrac{58+37}{414}=0.229468
\] Overall Accuracy \[
\text{Accuracy}=\dfrac{TP+TN}{N}=\dfrac{58+217}{414}=0.664251
\] Sensitivity \[
\text{Sensitivity}=\dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}=\dfrac{58}{58+37}=0.610526
\] Specificity \[
\text{Specificity}=\dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}=\dfrac{217}{217+102}=0.680251
\] Positive Predicted Value (Precision) \[
\text{Positive Predicted Value}=\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}=\dfrac{58}{58+102}=0.3625
\] Negative Predicted Value \[
\text{Negative Predicted Value}=\dfrac{TN}{TN+FN}=\dfrac{217}{37+217}=0.854331
\] Balanced Accuracy \[
\text{Balanced Accuracy}=\dfrac{Specificity+Sensitivity}{2}=\dfrac{0.664251+0.610526}{2}=0.637389
\] Cohen’s Kappa \[
P_0=\dfrac{TP+TN}{N}=\dfrac{58+217}{414}=0.664251
\] \[
P_e=\dfrac{(TP+FN)(TP+FP)+(FP+TN)(FN+TN)}{N^2}=\dfrac{(58+37)(58+102)+(102+217)(37+217)}{414^2}=0.561425
\] \[
\text{$\kappa$}=\dfrac{P_0-P_e}{1-P_e}=\dfrac{0.664251-0.561425}{1-0.561425}=0.234455
\] F1-Score \[
\text{F1-Score}=\dfrac{2TP}{2TP+FP+FN}=\dfrac{116}{116+102+37}=0.454902
\] ### Part b (2 points)
Read more of the Wikipedia article on Cohen’s Kappa, especially the Interpreting magnitude and the Limitations part. I cannot really verify that you did this, so this is on your honor.



Problem 2 (79 points)
The adult dataset (from the UC Irvine database) is one that is used to predict whether a person makes over $50K a year based on some other variables. The data came from the Census Bureau in 1994 and can be found in the Data folder in my Math3190_S26 GitHub repo. More info on the dataset can be found in the “adult.names” file.

Part a (4 points)
Read the data into R as a tibble, change the column names to be descriptive about what the variable in that column is, and change the one containing salary information to a factor. Read the “adult.names” file to see the column names. Note that even though this file has a .data. suffix, it is comma separated, so you can read it in using read.csv() in base R and the convert it to a tibble or read_csv() from the tidyverse, which wil read it in as a tibble.

library(tidyverse)

colnames(adult)=c("age","workclass","fnlwgt", "education","education_num","marital_staus","occupation","relationship","race","sex","capital_gain","capital_loss","hours_per_week","native_country","salary")
adult$salary=as.factor(adult$salary)



Part b (4 points)
Randomly split the dataset into a training and a testing group. Let’s use 4/5 of it for training and 1/5 for testing. You can do this with any function you’d like. Please set a seed with the set.seed() function before you do this so the results are reproducible.

set.seed(123)
n=nrow(adult)
train_index=sample(seq_len(n), size=0.8*n)
adult_train=adult[train_index, ]
adult_test=adult[-train_index, ]



Part c (5 points)
Fit two models for predicting whether a person’s salary is above $50K or not:
For the first model, fit a logistic regression model using something like glm(salary ~ age + education + race + sex + hours_per_week, data = adult_train, family = "binomial"). By the way, if you are unfamiliar with logistic regression, take some time to research it online or in my notes from Math 3150, which you can find here. We will deal with logistic regression again on the next homework. You don’t need to know everything about it, just learn a little about how it is used to predict the class for an observation. Of course, I’m happy to explain how it works in office hours as well.

log_model=glm(salary~age+education+race+sex+hours_per_week, data=adult_train, family="binomial")

For the second model, fit a \(k\) nearest neighbors model with \(k=7\) neighbors using the knn3() function in the caret package. Again, use age, education, race, sex, and hours_per_week as the predictors.

library(caret)

Loading required package: lattice



Attaching package: 'caret'


The following object is masked from 'package:purrr':

    lift

knn_model=knn3(salary~age+education+race+sex+hours_per_week, data=adult_train,k=7)



Part d (5 points)
With logistic regression, the most common cutoff value for the predicted probability for predicting a “success” is 0.5. Using 0.5 as this cutoff (above 0.5 should be labeled as “>50K”), obtain the class predictions and convert the variable to a factor. You can use the predict() function with type = "response" to obtain the predicted probabilities of being in the “>50K” group and then compare those probabilities to 0.5. Then use the confusionMatrix() function in the caret package to obtain the confusion matrix and many associated statistics. Print all of the output from that function. Remember to use the positive argument in that function. The positive should be the >50K category.

probabilities=predict(log_model,newdata=adult_test,type = "response")
class_predictions=ifelse(probabilities>0.5, ">50K", "<=50K")
class_predictions=factor(class_predictions,levels=levels(adult_test$salary))
matrix_log=confusionMatrix(data=class_predictions,reference=adult_test$salary,positive=">50K")
print(matrix_log)

Part e (4 points)
Obtain the class predictions for your kNN model and output the results of the confusionMatrix() function for this. Note that it will take a few seconds to obtain the predictions for the kNN model.

knn_predictions=predict(knn_model,newdata=adult_test,type="class")
matrix_knn=confusionMatrix(data=knn_predictions,reference=adult_test$salary,positive=">50K")
print(matrix_knn)

Part f (4 points)
Using the output from parts d and e, write a few sentences comparing and contrasting the strengths and weaknesses of each model when it comes to predictions.
Overall, the logistic regression and kNN models produced very similar performance metrics, likely due to the relationship between the predictors and salary being fairly linear. Because of this, data cleanly divides between the boundary of <=50K and >50K. So, the flexibility of kNN models do not make a noticble difference in preformance. The differences in each metric is within a few percentages, with the log model performing slighly better in overall accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity. The most noticible difference of around 8% is in positive predicted value which indicates the kNN model is producing more false positives in the >50K class.


Part g (8 points)
Using the train() function in the caret package, perform 5-fold cross validation for \(k\) in the kNN model using only the training set and again using age, education, race, sex, and hours_per_week as the predictors. Set the search for \(k\) to be from 1 to 21 (we’ll stop at 21 to save time) in the tuneGrid argument. Make sure to use the trControl option to set it to cross validation. Then use Cohen’s \(\kappa\) as the metric to determine the best \(k\) value. You can change the metric in the train() function to Cohen’s \(\kappa\) by putting in metric = "Kappa".
Then, if the best \(k\) is different than 7, fit another kNN model with the optimal \(k\) value. Please set a seed at the beginning of this code chunk.
It is fairly computationally expensive to optimize the \(k\) for the kNN model here since it takes so long to obtain the predictions. So, this may take a few minutes to run. Note that you can add #| cache: true to the top of the code chunk to cache the output of the code so the code doesn’t need to run each time you render the document.

set.seed(2)
train_knn=train(salary~age+education+race+sex+hours_per_week,data=adult_train, method="knn",trControl=trainControl(method="cv",number=5),tuneGrid=data.frame(k = 1:21), metric="Kappa")
print(train_knn)

knn_model_new=knn3(salary~age+education+race+sex+hours_per_week,data=adult_train,k=3)



Part h (20 points)
We mentioned the most common cutoff value for the predicted probability for predicting a “success” in logistic regression is 0.5. However, we can adjust this value to make it easier or more difficult to predict a success. Let’s optimize this cutoff value using 5-fold cross validation. Note: we could also do this with kNN, but we will not on this assignment.
Using the cutoff values from 0.15 to 0.85 by 0.05 (0.15, 0.20, 0.25, and so on up to 0.85) for predicting whether an adult has a salary above 50K, find which one performs best on the training set using the metric of Cohen’s \(\kappa\), which is given in the output of the confusionMatrix() function. You will need a couple loops here since the train() function cannot do this for us. Note: you can find the indices of the rows in each fold using the createFolds() function in the caret library. Please set a seed at the beginning of your code chunk for this part.

set.seed(3)
folds=createFolds(adult_train$salary,k=5,returnTrain=FALSE)
cutoffs=seq(0.15, 0.85,0.05)
mean_kappa=numeric(length(cutoffs))
for (c in seq_along(cutoffs)) {
  cutoff=cutoffs[c]
  kappa_vals=numeric(length(folds))
for (f in seq_along(folds)) {
    test_idx=folds[[f]]
    train_idx=setdiff(seq_len(nrow(adult_train)), test_idx)
    fold_train=adult_train[train_idx, ]
    fold_test=adult_train[test_idx, ]
    fold_log=glm(salary~age+education+race+sex+hours_per_week,data=fold_train,family=binomial)
    p_hat=predict(fold_log,newdata=fold_test,type="response")
    pred_class=ifelse(p_hat>cutoff, ">50K", "<=50K")
    pred_class=factor(pred_class,levels=levels(adult_train$salary))
    cm=confusionMatrix(data=pred_class,reference=fold_test$salary, positive = ">50K")
    kappa_vals[f]=as.numeric(cm$overall["Kappa"])}
mean_kappa[c]=mean(kappa_vals)}
results=data.frame(cutoff=cutoffs, mean_kappa=mean_kappa)
print(results)

Once you have your “optimal” cutoff value, repeat part d using this cutoff and compare the results of this output to the results of the output for a kNN model with the optimal \(k\) value you found in part g. The new cutoff for the logistic regression model significantly increased sensitivity from 0.3731 to 0.5788, balanced accuracy from 0.6538 to 0.7137, and the kappa value from 0.3593 to 0.4201. But, overall accuracy decreased slightly by just over a percent and specificity decreased fro, 0.9346 to 0.8487. ### Part j (15 points)
Finally, let’s test our two models (the logistic model with the “optimal” cutoff and the kNN model with the “optimal” \(k\)) on the test set. We must keep a few things in mind:

We must use the exact models we fit to the training set. We aren’t fitting new models here, we are just getting predictions from the test set using these models we already fit. You fit the logistic regression model in part c (with the best cutoff probability determined in part h) and you fit the kNN model in either part c or part g.
We should not use the results of the testing predictions to change our models. That should have been done with the training sets.

Find the predictions for the test set using both models, print the output of the confusionMatrix() function for each model, and compare the results in a few sentences.

probabilities=predict(log_model, newdata=adult_test, type="response")
log_test=ifelse(probabilities>best_cutoff, ">50K", "<=50K")
log_test=factor(log_test, levels=levels(adult_test$salary))
matrix_log=confusionMatrix(data=log_test,reference=adult_test$salary,positive=">50K")
print(matrix_log)

The overall accuracy for the two models is nearly identical. The logistic model, however, resulted in a much higher kappa value, sensistivity, and balanced accuracy. While, the kNN model resulted in a higher specificity and positive predicted value. The logistic regression model was therefore ideal for identifying those earning over 50K, and the kNN model was ideal for identifying those earning under 50K.


Part k (5 points)
Even though one method may be better on a given dataset than another, that does not mean that method will always predict better. However, logistic regression has a few advantages over kNN regardless of predictive power. List at least three advantages logistic regression has over kNN.

Logistic regression models offer more interpretability. Each predictor in the model’s equation has a coefficient with a specific meaning so it is clear wich variables most effect the decision.
They are more stable because they are a global model and are therefore less effected by small changes in data. kNN is a local method. Because it’s dependent on its nearest neighbors, it is sensitve to small changes.
They are better for dealing with class imbalances. The cutoff value can be raised or lowered based on which class you want to focus on. Whereas, kNN neighbordhoods are more likeley to predict the majority class in the neighborhood and are less senistive to minorities.

title: MATH 3190 Homework 10
text:
Note that your homework should be completed in Quarto. You can just add your answers and code to this document in the appropriate part. The file should be rendered as an html document or pdf document (html is quicker to render and you will need an installation of LaTeX to render as a pdf). You will “turn in” this homework by uploading the files, the .qmd and output file, to your GitHub Math_3190_Assignment repository in the Homework/Homework_10 directory. Make sure all supporting files (if there are any) are in this directory as well. You should use git and Unix commands in a terminal for this (i.e. don’t manually upload files to GitHub). In addition, you will submit just your html or pdf file on Canvas to unlock the solutions.
Don’t forget to also put a self-critique comment on Canvas indicating what you did correctly and incorrectly and include any questions you may have about the assignment after submitting the assignment and thoroughly looking through the solutions.

Problem 1 (45 points)
In the itsmr package is the dataset called wine that contains 142 accounts of Australian red wine sales per month from January 1980 to October 1991.

Part a (5 points)
Load in this data and convert it to a tsibble. You can do this by first making it a ts object by typing ts(wine, deltat = 1/12, start = 1980) and then piping that into the as_tsibble() function in the tsibble package.
Then use ggplot() to plot the time series. Show both the points and lines connecting them. On the \(x\) axis, put each year using the scale_x_yearmonth() function and give the plot a title.

library(itsmr)
library(ggplot2)
library(tsibble)


Attaching package: 'tsibble'


The following objects are masked from 'package:base':

    intersect, setdiff, union

wine=ts(wine, deltat=1/12, start=1980) |> 
  as_tsibble()

ggplot(wine, aes(x=index, y=value))+
  geom_line()+
  geom_point()+
  scale_x_yearmonth(date_breaks="1 year", date_labels= "%y")+labs(x="Year", y="Sales", title="Wine Sales by Year")










Part b (2 points)
Remove the last 34 rows from the wine data. We will use them to compare how our forecasts look later.

wine_new=wine[1:(nrow(wine)-34),]



Part c (5 points)
Use the gg_season() plot in the ggtime library to plot the seasonality. Does it seem like there is trend and/or seasonality in this time series? If so, explain the patterns.

library(ggtime)
gg_season(wine_new, y=value, labels="both")+labs(x="Month", y="Sales", title="Seasonality")








Each year sales are the lowest in Janurary, then steadily rise till they peak July and August and slightly drop again. Each year the amount of sales as a whole also increases.


Part d (4 points)
Use the adf.test() function in the tseries library to perform a Dickey-Fuller test to determine if we have evidence this time series is stationary. Only the column with the sales should be entered. Since we have data by month, use the k = 12 option. Comment on the results of the test.

library(tseries)|> suppressPackageStartupMessages()
adf.test(wine_new$value, k=12)


    Augmented Dickey-Fuller Test

data:  wine_new$value
Dickey-Fuller = -1.6093, Lag order = 12, p-value = 0.7387
alternative hypothesis: stationary


The p-value is greater than 0.05 so we fail to reject the null hypothesis, which means the time series is not stationary.


Part e (6 points)
Difference the data once. Take \(Y_t = Y_{t+1} - Y_{t}\) for \(t=1,\dots,n-1\). In the notes, I defined the differences as \(Y_t = Y_{t} - Y_{t+1}\), but it actually does not matter. Plot this differenced time series (you can just put the index on the x-axis) and use the adf.test() function again on the differenced data. Again, use k = 12 option. Comment on the results of the test.

wine_diff=wine_new$value[-1]-wine_new$value[-length(wine_new$value)]

adf.test(wine_diff, k=12)

Warning in adf.test(wine_diff, k = 12): p-value smaller than printed p-value



    Augmented Dickey-Fuller Test

data:  wine_diff
Dickey-Fuller = -5.0592, Lag order = 12, p-value = 0.01
alternative hypothesis: stationary

wine_diff=ts(wine_diff, deltat=1/12, start=1980) |> 
  as_tsibble()

ggplot(wine_diff, aes(x=index, y=value))+
  geom_line()+
  geom_point()+
  scale_x_yearmonth(date_breaks="1 year", date_labels= "%y")+labs(x="Year", y="Sales", title="Differenced Wine Sales")








The P value is less than 0.05 so we reject the null hypothesis, which means the time series is now stationary.


Part f (4 points)
Now use the auto.arima() function in the forecast library to fit an ARIMA model to this time series (put the entire original tsibble into this function, not the differences time series).
Feel free to check out this resource that discusses more about time series, including what the drift term is.

library(forecast) |> suppressPackageStartupMessages()
wine_mod=auto.arima(wine_new)
wine_mod

Series: wine_new 
ARIMA(1,0,1)(0,1,1)[12] with drift 

Coefficients:
         ar1      ma1     sma1   drift
      0.9144  -0.7519  -0.7247  9.0791
s.e.  0.0918   0.1356   0.1663  1.6526

sigma^2 = 33825:  log likelihood = -639.11
AIC=1288.23   AICc=1288.9   BIC=1301.05




Part g (5 points)
Use the forecast() function from the forecast library to predict the future for 34 months. You should specify you are using the forecast library by typing forecast::forecast() since the itsmr library also has a forecast() function. The input of this function is your model you fit in part (f). Use the model fit to the undifferenced data.
Then use the autoplot() function to plot the forecast.

wine_forecast=forecast::forecast(wine_mod, level=95, h=34)
autoplot(wine_forecast)










Part h (8 points)
Plot the actual values for 1989, 1990, and 1991 along with the predicted values from the forecast and a shaded 95% interval bands. The geom_ribbon() function may be useful here. Is there any point where the true value is outside the interval? If so, report the year(s) and month(s) where that is the case.

wine_last=wine[(nrow(wine)-33):nrow(wine),]
forecast_df=data.frame(index=wine_last$index, mean=as.numeric(wine_forecast$mean), lower=as.numeric(wine_forecast$lower[,1]), upper=as.numeric(wine_forecast$upper[,1]))

ggplot()+
  geom_line(data=wine_last, aes(x=index, y=value))+
  geom_line(data=forecast_df, aes(x=index, y=mean), linetype="dashed", color="red")+
  geom_ribbon(data=forecast_df, aes(x=index, ymin=lower, ymax=upper), alpha=0.2, fill="blue")+
  scale_x_yearmonth(date_breaks="3 months", date_labels= "%b %y")+labs(x="Year", y="Sales", title="Predicted VS Actual Sales")








The actual values fall within the confidence interval for the most part. However, during April 1991, the true value is clearly outside the interval.


Part i (6 points)
Use the checkresiduals() function in the forecast library to create a residual plot, an autocorrelation plot, and a histogram for the residuals. The input to this function should be your model fit with the auto.arima() function. Comment on whether the assumptions seem to be satisfied here. If you’d like, you can make a QQ-plot as well, but the histogram contains the same information.
Note: this function will also give you information from the Ljung-Box test, which tests if the residuals exhibit autocorrelation. A large p-value means we have no evidence of autocorrelation.

checkresiduals(wine_mod, lag=12, plot=TRUE)









    Ljung-Box test

data:  Residuals from ARIMA(1,0,1)(0,1,1)[12] with drift
Q* = 13.316, df = 9, p-value = 0.1488

Model df: 3.   Total lags used: 12


The residuals shows no clear trend and seem randomly scattered. The ACF plot shows that correlations are within the bounds of significance. The histogram is apporximately normal. The Ljung_Box test gives a large p-value indicating no evidence of autocorrelation.

title: MATH 3190 Homework 9
text:
Note that your homework should be completed in Quarto. You can just add your answers and code to this document in the appropriate part. The file should be rendered as an html document or pdf document (html is quicker to render and you will need an installation of LaTeX to render as a pdf). You will “turn in” this homework by uploading the files, the .qmd and output file, to your GitHub Math_3190_Assignment repository in the Homework/Homework_9 directory. Make sure all supporting files (if there are any) are in this directory as well. You should use git and Unix commands in a terminal for this (i.e. don’t manually upload files to GitHub). In addition, you will submit just your html or pdf file on Canvas to unlock the solutions.
Don’t forget to also put a self-critique comment on Canvas indicating what you did correctly and incorrectly and include any questions you may have about the assignment after submitting the assignment and thoroughly looking through the solutions.

Problem 1 (70 points)
Before we begin, please re-install the math3190package since there was an update to the classifier_plot() function that is needed on this assignment. Use either devtools::install_github("rbrown53/math3190package") or
remotes::install_github("rbrown53/math3190package").
The National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases conducted a study on 768 adult female Pima Native Americans living near Phoenix. The purpose of the study was to investigate factors related to diabetes. The response variable in this study (test) was whether or not the patient showed signs of diabetes (1 = yes, 0 = no). One explanatory variable considered was the plasma glucose concentration (in mg/dL) at 2 hours in an oral glucose tolerance test (glucose). Many other potential explanatory variables were considered as well including body mass index (BMI). The goal here is to predict the diabetes status of the subject, which is in the results variable.

Part a (5 points)
Install and/or load the faraway package with the pima dataset. Set a seed and then split the data into a testing and a training set with the testing set containing 20% of the data values. When you do this, change the name of the variable “test” to “result” since it will be a bit confusing to call that test when we have a test dataset. Then make the result variable a factor so R treats it as categorical rather than numeric.

library(faraway)
data(pima)
set.seed(1)

test_index=sample(1:nrow(pima), size=round(0.2*nrow(pima)))
names(pima)[names(pima)=="test"]="result"
pimatrain=pima[-test_index, ]
pimatest=pima[test_index, ]

pimatrain$result=as.factor(pimatrain$result)
pimatest$result=as.factor(pimatest$result)



Part b (6 points)

Fit a logistic regression model to the training set for predicting result from the glucose and bmi variables.
Then use the classifier_plot() function in the math3190package (make sure you re-installed it!) to make a plot that shows the regions on the glucose-bmi-plane that are predicted to be 0 and 1. This shows how logistic regression divides the classes. Use the training set as the data input in this function.
Next, obtain the predictions for the test set with a probability cutoff of 0.5 to predict if the person has diabetes. Create a confusion matrix using the confusionMatrix() function in the caret library. Make sure to put positive = "1" for this and all other confusion matrices on this assignment. Print all of the output from that function that shows the confusion matrix and many associated statistics.
Write a sentence or two discussing how well this model performs in predicting on the test set.


library(math3190package) |> suppressPackageStartupMessages()
library(caret) |> suppressPackageStartupMessages()
pimamod=glm(result~glucose+bmi, pimatrain, family="binomial")
classifier_plot(pimamod, pimatrain)







probability=predict(pimamod, newdata=pimatest, type="response")
predictions=ifelse(probability>0.5,1,0)
predictions=as.factor(predictions)
confusionMatrix(predictions, pimatest$result, positive="1")

Confusion Matrix and Statistics

          Reference
Prediction  0  1
         0 90 29
         1 12 23
                                          
               Accuracy : 0.7338          
                 95% CI : (0.6566, 0.8017)
    No Information Rate : 0.6623          
    P-Value [Acc > NIR] : 0.03492         
                                          
                  Kappa : 0.3529          
                                          
 Mcnemar's Test P-Value : 0.01246         
                                          
            Sensitivity : 0.4423          
            Specificity : 0.8824          
         Pos Pred Value : 0.6571          
         Neg Pred Value : 0.7563          
             Prevalence : 0.3377          
         Detection Rate : 0.1494          
   Detection Prevalence : 0.2273          
      Balanced Accuracy : 0.6623          
                                          
       'Positive' Class : 1               
                                          


This model had an overall accuracy of 73% on the test set. However, its sensitivity was only 44% so it did a poor job at catching postive cases, but its specificity was 88% so it predicted negative cases much better.


Part c (3 points)
Now let’s use naïve Bayes to get predictions. Use the naive_bayes() function in the naivebayes library to fit a model predicting result using glucose and bmi. Find the predicted probabilities of being a 0 and a 1 for the first observation in the training set.

library(naivebayes)|> suppressPackageStartupMessages()
bayesmod=naive_bayes(result~glucose+bmi, data=pimatrain)
head(predict(bayesmod, newdata=pimatrain, type="prob"),1)

Warning: predict.naive_bayes(): more features in the newdata are provided as
there are probability tables in the object. Calculation is performed based on
features to be found in the tables.


             0          1
[1,] 0.9230419 0.07695808




Part d (9 points)
Now find the predicted probability of being a 0 and a 1 for the first observation in the training set “by hand” like we did in the notes on pages 187 and 188. To simplify this a bit, let’s again only use the variables glucose and bmi here. Of course, use R to help you (the dnorm() function and some tidyverse functions will be especially nice), but don’t use the naive_bayes() function here. Note: these variables we are using to predict are numeric, so we will assume a normal distribution for both of them (maybe not a good assumption, but let’s go with it). After you get the probabilities, compare them to the ones you got in part (c). They should match.

x=pimatrain[1,]
p0=mean(pimatrain$result=="0")
p1=mean(pimatrain$result=="1")

t0=filter(pimatrain, result=="0")
t1=filter(pimatrain, result=="1")

g0_mean=mean(t0$glucose)
g0_sd=sd(t0$glucose)
b0_mean=mean(t0$bmi)
b0_sd=sd(t0$bmi)

g1_mean=mean(t1$glucose)
g1_sd=sd(t1$glucose)
b1_mean=mean(t1$bmi)
b1_sd=sd(t1$bmi)

l_g0=dnorm(x$glucose, g0_mean, g0_sd)
l_b0=dnorm(x$bmi, b0_mean, b0_sd)
l_g1=dnorm(x$glucose, g1_mean, g1_sd)
l_b1=dnorm(x$bmi, b1_mean, b1_sd)

num0=p0*l_g0*l_b0
num1=p1*l_g1*l_b1

prob_0=num0/(num0+num1)
prob_1=num1/(num0+num1)

prob_0

[1] 0.9230419

prob_1

[1] 0.07695808


My probabilities in part c and part d match exactly.


Part e (3 points)
Now fit a naïve Bayes model predicting result from glucose and bmi using a kernel instead of assuming a normal distribution. You can do this by setting usekernel = TRUE in the naive_bayes() function.

bayesmod2=naive_bayes(result~glucose+bmi, data=pimatrain, usekernel=TRUE)



Part f (5 points)
Now use the classifier_plot() function to make two plots: one using the first naïve Bayes from part (c) and one using the naïve Bayes with the kernel from part (e). Both should show the separation on the training set. Comment on how these models seems to perform in separating the results classes and if there seems like there may be any over-fitting here.

classifier_plot(bayesmod, pimatrain)







classifier_plot(bayesmod2, pimatrain)








The first naive bayes plot has a single, smooth boundary, while the model with the kernel produced a more irregular, flexible boundary. The second model seems to be overfit and sensitive to noise.


Part g (5 points)
Report the output of the confusionMatrix() function for both naïve Bayes models when predicting on the test set. Write a couple sentences comparing the confusion matrix statistics.

pred1=predict(bayesmod, pimatest)

Warning: predict.naive_bayes(): more features in the newdata are provided as
there are probability tables in the object. Calculation is performed based on
features to be found in the tables.

pred2=predict(bayesmod2, pimatest)

Warning: predict.naive_bayes(): more features in the newdata are provided as
there are probability tables in the object. Calculation is performed based on

confusionMatrix(pred1, pimatest$result, positive="1")

Confusion Matrix and Statistics

                 95% CI : (0.6566, 0.8017)
    No Information Rate : 0.6623          
    P-Value [Acc > NIR] : 0.03492         
                                          
                  Kappa : 0.3529          
                                          
 Mcnemar's Test P-Value : 0.01246

            Specificity : 0.8824

confusionMatrix(pred2, pimatest$result, positive="1")

The values are very similar overall. The standard naive bayes model has a slightly higher overall accuracy of 73.4%, compared to 72.7% with the other model. The standard model also has a slightly higher specificity of 88.2% compared to 86.3% with the other model. The second model has a slightly higher sensitivity, on the other hand, of 46.2% compared to 44.3% with the standard model.


Part h (3 points)
Now let’s use a support vector machine (SVM) for classifying diabetes using all other variables. We’ll use the svm() function in the e1071 library. Fit a model with a polynomial kernel here with degree 3 (the default), a cost of 1 (also the default), and keep scale = TRUE (which is the default as well). Ideally, we would do a cross validation to select the cost, but we’ll do that for the next kernel.

library(e1071)


Attaching package: 'e1071'


The following object is masked from 'package:ggplot2':

    element

svmmod=svm(result~glucose+bmi, data=pimatrain, kernel="polynomial")



Part i (10 points)
Now let’s use a different kernel. Let’s use the radial kernel for the SVM. This has two tuning parameters: the cost and \(\gamma\). Let’s use the train() function in the caret package to do 5-fold cross validation (set a seed, please) to select both of those parameters. However, the train() function uses \(\sigma\) instead of \(\gamma\) and \(C\) instead of cost.
Use the trainControl() function with the trControl option and the tuneGrid option in the train() function to perform this cross-validation. For sigma, use seq(0.1, 2, by = 0.1) and for C also use seq(0.1, 2, by = 0.1). Put method = "svmRadial" in the train() function as well so it knows to use a SVM with the radial kernel. You will need to install the kernlab package for this to work. Set a seed here as well.
By default, the “best” sigma and C values will be chosen with accuracy instead of Cohen’s kappa. Make sure to choose the best values using kappa (although both metrics may choose the same “best” values).

ctrltrain=trainControl(method="cv", number=5)
grid=expand.grid(sigma=seq(0.1,2,by=0.1), C=seq(0.1,2,by=0.1))
set.seed(1)
svmmod2=train(result~glucose+bmi, data=pimatrain, method="svmRadial", trControl=ctrltrain, tuneGrid=grid, metric="kappa")

Warning in train.default(x, y, weights = w, ...): The metric "kappa" was not in
the result set. Accuracy will be used instead.

svmmod2$bestTune

    sigma   C
385     2 0.5




Part j (3 points)
Fit the SVM with the radial kernel using the svm() function and the cross-validated cost and \(\gamma\) found in part (i).

bestsvm=svm(result~glucose+bmi, data=pimatrain, kernel="radial", cost=svmmod2$bestTune$C, gamma=svmmod2$bestTune$sigma)



Part k (5 points)
Create the classifier plots for both SVM models and plot the training set and comment on how well these seem to separate the classes.

classifier_plot(svmmod, pimatrain)







classifier_plot(bestsvm, pimatrain)








The plot for the first SVM model has one continuous boundary that separates the data pretty well. The plot for the second SVM model has multiple, localized boundaries that classify data very similarly.


Part l (3 points)
Report the confusion matrix statistics for both SVM models when predicting the test set.

pred1=predict(svmmod, pimatest)
pred2=predict(bestsvm, pimatest)

confusionMatrix(pred1, pimatest$result, positive="1")

                  Kappa : 0.1902          
                                          
 Mcnemar's Test P-Value : 2.479e-09

            Specificity : 0.98039         
         Pos Pred Value : 0.81818         
         Neg Pred Value : 0.69930         
             Prevalence : 0.33766         
         Detection Rate : 0.05844

confusionMatrix(pred2, pimatest$result, positive="1")

                 95% CI : (0.6497, 0.7958)

            Specificity : 0.8627

         Neg Pred Value : 0.7586          
             Prevalence : 0.3377          
         Detection Rate : 0.1558          
   Detection Prevalence : 0.2468          
      Balanced Accuracy : 0.6621          
                                          
       'Positive' Class : 1               
                                          




Part m (10 points)
Compare the output of the confusionMarix() function for all of our classifiers: the logistic regression model, the naïve Bayes with a normal assumption, the naïve Bayes using kernel density estimation, the SVM with a polynomial kernel, and the SVM using a radial kernel. Which was best? Which was worst? How different were the results? You can focus on the Cohen’s \(\kappa\) and balanced accuracy for each one.
For the logistic regression model: kappa was 0.3529, and balanced accuracy was 0.6623. For the first naive bayes, kappa was 0.3529, and balanced accuracy was 0.6623. For the second naive bayes, kappa was 0.3472, and balanced accuracy was 0.6621. For the polynomial SVM model: kappa was 0.1902, and balanced accuracy was 0.57673. For the radial SVM model: kappa was 0.3472, and balanced accuracy was 0.6621. The results were very similar. Overall, it seemed that the logistic regression and the first naive bayes models preformed the best with the highest kappa and balanced accuracy values. The polynomial SVM model preformed the worst wih the lowest kappa and balanced accuracy values.

title: MATH 3190 Homework 1
text:
Note that your homework should be completed in Quarto. You can just add your answers and code to this document in the appropriate part. The file should be rendered as an html document or pdf document (html is quicker to render and you will need an installation of LaTeX to render as a pdf). You will “turn in” this homework by uploading the files, the .qmd and output file, to your GitHub Math_3190_Assignment repository in the Homework/Homework_1 directory. Make sure all supporting files (if there are any) are in this directory as well. You should use git and Unix commands in a terminal for this (i.e. don’t manually upload files to GitHub). In addition, you will submit just your html or pdf file on Canvas to unlock the solutions.
Don’t forget to also put a self-critique comment on Canvas indicating what you did correctly and incorrectly and include any questions you may have about the assignment after submitting the assignment and thoroughly looking through the solutions.

Problem 1 (18 points)
In two different code chunks, write two functions called gghist and ggbox. The first code chunk is already given below, but you’ll need to add the second one. The gghist function should create a ggplot histogram of a variable that is given as a vector. The ggbox function should create a ggplot box plot when a single numeric vector is given or it should create side-by-side box plots if one numeric and one categorical variables are given. You can check if a vector is numeric with the is.numeric() function. Allow the user to indicate whether it should be horizontal or vertical box plots. Make sure you set echo and eval both to true for these code chunks. Also make sure to label the code chunks.

library(ggplot2)

gghist <- function(vec) {
  df <- data.frame(x = vec)
  
  ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +
    geom_histogram()
}


ggbox=function(x, group=NULL, horizontal= FALSE){
  if (!is.numeric(x)){
    stop("x must be numeric")
  }
  if (is.null(group)){df=data.frame(x=x)
    plot=ggplot(df,aes(y=x))+geom_boxplot()
  }else{
    df=data.frame(x=x, group=group)
    p=ggplot(df, aes(x=group, y=x))+geom_boxplot()
  }
  if (horizontal) {p=p +coord_flip()}
return(p)}



Problem 2 (57 points)

Part a (6 points)
Learn about the built-in read.fwf() R function for use in downloading data from a URL into R. The widths and strip.white options will be especially useful here. Part j (7 points)
Generalize this by writing a function that will do this for a given team, and create a tibble with this information for all teams. Arrange this tibble first by winning percentage (descending) and by team (alphabetically) if there are any ties when it comes to winning percentage. A for loop and the add_row() function may be useful here.

team_summary <- function(data, team) {
  data |>
    filter(home_team == team | away_team == team) |>
    mutate(
      result = case_when(
        home_team == team & score_difference > 0 ~ "win",
        home_team == team & score_difference < 0 ~ "loss",
        away_team == team & score_difference < 0 ~ "win",
        away_team == team & score_difference > 0 ~ "loss",
        TRUE ~ NA_character_
      )
    ) |>
    summarize(
      team = team,
      wins = sum(result == "win", na.rm = TRUE),
      losses = sum(result == "loss", na.rm = TRUE)
    ) |>
    mutate(winning_pct = wins / (wins + losses))
}

teams <- sort(unique(c(cbbga_2025$home_team, cbbga_2025$away_team)))

records <- tibble(
  team = character(),
  wins = integer(),
  losses = integer(),
  winning_pct = double()
)

for (value in teams) {

  records <- add_row(
    records,
    team = team_record$team,
    wins = team_record$wins,
    losses = team_record$losses,
    winning_pct = team_record$winning_pct
  )
}

records <- records |>

Note, this is a little challenging. teams_2 <- c("Utah Tech", "Southern Utah")
points <- cbbga_2025 |> filter(home_team %in% teams_2 | away_team %in% teams_2) |>
pivot_longer(cols = c(away_team, home_team),names_to = "side",values_to = "team") |>
mutate(points = if_else(side == "away_team", away_score, home_score)) |>
filter(team %in% teams_2) |>select(team, points)
ggbox <- function(x, group = NULL, horizontal = FALSE) {
if (!is.numeric(x)) {
    stop("x must be numeric")}
if (is.null(group)) {
    df <- data.frame(x = x)
    p <- ggplot(df, aes(y = x)) +
      geom_boxplot(fill = "firebrick", color = "black")
      } else {
    df <- data.frame(x = x, group = group)
    p <- ggplot(df, aes(x = group, y = x)) +
      geom_boxplot(fill = "firebrick", color = "black")}
if (horizontal) {
    p <- p + coord_flip()}
return(p)}

title: MATH 3190 Homework 6
text:
Note that your homework should be completed in Quarto. You can just add your answers and code to this document in the appropriate part. The file should be rendered as an html document or pdf document (html is quicker to render and you will need an installation of LaTeX to render as a pdf). You will “turn in” this homework by uploading the files, the .qmd and output file, to your GitHub Math_3190_Assignment repository in the Homework/Homework_6 directory. Make sure all supporting files (if there are any) are in this directory as well. You should use git and Unix commands in a terminal for this (i.e. don’t manually upload files to GitHub). In addition, you will submit just your html or pdf file on Canvas to unlock the solutions.
Don’t forget to also put a self-critique comment on Canvas indicating what you did correctly and incorrectly and include any questions you may have about the assignment after submitting the assignment and thoroughly looking through the solutions.

Problem 1 (28 points)
An automobile consulting company wants to understand the factors on which the pricing of cars depends. The dataset car_price_prediction in the math3190package (and also found in the GitHub data folder) has information on the sales of 4340 vehicles.

Part a (3 points)
Create new variables in the dataset by taking the natural log of selling price, year, and km_driven. This will help linearize the association between variables. Then fit a linear model for predicting the log of selling price using all other variables except “name”, “year”, “selling_price”, and “km_driven” (the logged variables should be used instread of those last three). Also, there is only one vehicle with a fuel type of “Electric”. Remove that vehicle from the dataset.

library(math3190package) |> suppressPackageStartupMessages()
car_price_prediction=car_price_prediction |> mutate(log_price=log(selling_price), log_year=log(year), log_km=log(km_driven)) |> filter(fuel != "Electric")
car_mod=lm(log_price ~ log_year+log_km+fuel+seller_type+transmission+owner, data=car_price_prediction)

When \(\lambda=0\), the coefficients match those of the OLS model and all predictors are present. \(\lambda=0.01\), the coffecients shrink toward zero but most of the predictors remain in the model. When \(\lambda=0.1\), the coefficients shrink even closer to zero and many of the predictors are removed from the model. Finally, when \(\lambda=1\), only the intercept remains and all coefficients become zero.
Note: when \(\lambda=0\), the LASSO coefficients should equal the OLS model coefficients. However, they will actually be a bit off here. That is because the glmnet() function has a thresh argument that sets a threshold for convergence. It is, by default, set to 1e-07. To make the parameters match, you can change that to thresh = 1e-14 instead. This is not necessary, though.

title: MATH 3190 Homework 8
text:
Note that your homework should be completed in Quarto. You can just add your answers and code to this document in the appropriate part. The file should be rendered as an html document or pdf document (html is quicker to render and you will need an installation of LaTeX to render as a pdf). You will “turn in” this homework by uploading the files, the .qmd and output file, to your GitHub Math_3190_Assignment repository in the Homework/Homework_8 directory. Make sure all supporting files (if there are any) are in this directory as well. You should use git and Unix commands in a terminal for this (i.e. don’t manually upload files to GitHub). In addition, you will submit just your html or pdf file on Canvas to unlock the solutions.
Don’t forget to also put a self-critique comment on Canvas indicating what you did correctly and incorrectly and include any questions you may have about the assignment after submitting the assignment and thoroughly looking through the solutions.

Problem 1 (10 points)
Three airlines serve a small town in Ohio. Airline A has 52% of all scheduled flights, airline B has 35% and airline C has the remaining 13%. Their on-time rates are 85%, 67%, and 41%, respectively. A flight just left on-time. What is the probability that it was a flight of airline A?
\(P(A)=0.52, P(T|A)=0.85\) \(P(B)=0.35, P(T|B)=0.67\) \(P(C)=0.13, P(T|C)=0.41\) \(P(A|T)=\dfrac{(0.52)(0.85)}{(0.52)(0.85)+(.35)(0.67)+(0.13)(0.41)}=0.6056=61\%\)


Problem 2 (15 points)
Suppose we have a data set with each observation \(x_i\) independent and identically exponentially distributed for \(i=1,2,\dots,n\). That is, \(x_i\sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)\) where \(\lambda\) is the rate parameter. We would like to find a posterior (or at least a function proportional to it) for \(\lambda\).

Part a (6 points)
Write down the likelihood function (or a function proportional to it) in this situation. We would call this \(p(x|\lambda)\).
\(p(x_i|\lambda)=\lambda e^{-\lambda x_i}\)
\(p(\boldsymbol{x}|\lambda)\propto \lambda^n e^{-\lambda \sum_{i=1}^n x_i}\)


Part b (6 points)
Now let \(\lambda\) have a normal prior with mean 0.1 and variance 1: \(\lambda\sim N(1/10,\ 1)\). Use this and the likelihood from part (a) to write down a function that is proportional to the posterior of \(\lambda\) given \(\boldsymbol{x}\). We call this \(p(\lambda|\boldsymbol{x})\).
\(p(\lambda)\propto e^{{-(\lambda-1/10)^2}/2}\)
\(p(\lambda|\boldsymbol{x})\propto \lambda^n e^{-\lambda \sum_{i=1}^n x_i} e^{{-(\lambda-1/10)^2}/2}=\lambda^n e^{-\lambda \sum_{i=1}^n x_i -(\lambda-1/10)^2/2}\)


Part c (3 points)
Which would be more appropriate here to obtain samples of \(\lambda\), the Gibbs or Metropolis algorithm? Explain why. You may want to look on page 171 of Notes 9 in the conjugate prior table.
From the table, an exponential likelihood has a gamma conjugate prior, but this prior is normal. So the posterior is not in a known form. Metropolis sempling is a better choice since the full distribution is unknown.



Problem 3 (30 points)
Suppose we have the vector x = c(1.83, 1.72, 2.13, 2.49, 0.90, 2.01, 1.51, 3.12, 1.29, 1.54, 2.94, 3.02, 0.93, 2.78) that we believe comes from a gamma distribution with shape of 10 and some rate \(\beta\): \(x_i\sim\text{Gam}(10,\ \beta)\). We will use sampling to obtain some information about \(\beta\). Let’s put a gamma prior on \(\beta\) with a shape of \(\alpha_0\) and a rate of 1: \(\beta\sim\text{Gam}(\alpha_0,\ 1)\).

Part a (5 points)
Use the fact that this is a conjugate prior to write down what kind of distribution the posterior of \(\beta\), which is \(p(\beta|\boldsymbol{x})\), is.
\(p(\beta|\boldsymbol{x})\sim Gamma(\alpha_0+10n, 1+\sum_{i=1}^{10} x_i)\)


Part b (5 points)
Let \(\alpha_0=1\). In an R code chunk, sample 10,000 \(\beta\) values from the distribution you wrote down in part (a) using the rgamma() function and report the 95% credible interval for \(\beta\) using the 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles.

x = c(1.83, 1.72, 2.13, 2.49, 0.90, 2.01, 1.51, 3.12, 1.29, 1.54, 2.94, 3.02, 0.93, 2.78)
shape1=1+10*length(x)
rate=1+sum(x)
beta1=rgamma(10000, shape1, rate)
quantile(beta1, c(0.025, 0.975))

    2.5%    97.5% 
4.083028 5.631757 




Part c (3 points)
Repeat part (b) with \(\alpha_0 = 10\).

shape2=10+10*length(x)
beta2=rgamma(10000, shape2, rate)
quantile(beta2, c(0.025, 0.975))

    2.5%    97.5% 
4.362654 5.993550 




Part d (3 points)
Repeat part (b) with \(\alpha_0 = 100\).

shape3=100+10*length(x)
beta3=rgamma(10000, shape3, rate)
quantile(beta3, c(0.025, 0.975))

    2.5%    97.5% 
7.213157 9.282164 




Part e (4 points)
Comment on the effect the value of \(\alpha_0\) has on the credible intervals for \(\beta\) as revealed in parts (b), (c), and (d).
Increasing \(\alpha_0\) shifts the entire credible interval shifts to the right towards greater values of \(\beta\).


Part f (6 points)
Now suppose we have twice as much data (given in the R code chunk below). Repeat parts (b), (c), and (d) using this x vector given below instead of the other one and report the three 95% credible intervals. Note, this new vector x will change the shape and rate parameters used in the rgamma() functions.

x <- c(1.83, 1.72, 2.13, 2.49, 0.90, 2.01, 1.51, 3.12, 1.29, 1.54,
       2.94, 3.02, 0.93, 2.78, 2.76, 1.70, 1.42, 2.16, 1.07, 2.21,
       2.38, 2.27, 1.72, 1.44, 1.54, 1.72, 1.87, 1.39)


shape1=1+10*length(x)
rate=1+sum(x)
beta1=rgamma(10000, shape1, rate)
quantile(beta1, c(0.025, 0.975))

    2.5%    97.5% 
4.549915 5.734224 

shape2=10+10*length(x)
beta2=rgamma(10000, shape2, rate)
quantile(beta2, c(0.025, 0.975))

    2.5%    97.5% 
4.707196 5.920591 

shape3=100+10*length(x)
beta3=rgamma(10000, shape3, rate)
quantile(beta3, c(0.025, 0.975))

    2.5%    97.5% 
6.257608 7.642663 




Part g (4 points)
In this problem, the true \(\beta\) value is 5. Write a sentence or two about the effect adding more data has to these credible intervals by comparing the intervals from parts (b)-(d) to the intervals from part (f).
When more data is used, the credible intervals become more narrow, increasing the certainty in the estimate for \(\beta\). For example, when \(\alpha_0\) is 1, the interval for the original data set has a range of \(1.5\). With twice as much data, the range decreases to \(1.2\). The intervals also vary less with an increase in the value of \(\alpha_0\). With the original data set, the lower interval bound increased from \(4.07\) to \(7.20\). With twice as much data, the the lower bound only increased from \(4.54\) to \(6.24\). In both data sets, the true value of \(\beta\) was not included in the interval when \(\alpha_0=100\).



Problem 4 (35 points)
Let’s apply the Bayesian framework to a regression problem. In the math3190package library is the treeseeds dataset (it is also on GitHub) that contains information about species of tree, the count of seeds it produces, and the average weight of those seeds in mg.

Part a (3 points)
Using the treeseeds data, create new variables defined by taking the log of the counts and weights. Fit an OLS regression model using logged weight to predict logged count.

library(math3190package) |> suppressPackageStartupMessages()
tree <- treeseeds |>
  mutate(lcount = log(count),
         lweight = log(weight))

# Fit model here
treemod=lm(lcount~lweight, data=tree)



Part b (5 points)
Please read this carefully and try to understand what is being done here. There is just one question at the end of this part for you. We will walk through the mathematics and code of obtaining the posterior and samples together here since this problem will focus on looking at diagnostics, but I want you to see the Metropolis algorithm in action! Make sure to read this in the html or pdf file where all equations are rendered nicely.
Assuming the true errors are normal with mean 0 and variance \(\sigma^2\), \(\epsilon_i\sim N(0,\ \sigma^2)\), it can be shown that each \(y_i\) has the distribution \[
p(y_i|x_i,\beta_0,\beta_1\sigma^2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}\exp\left(
-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}(y_i-\beta_0-\beta_1x_i)^2\right)
\]
So, we can write the likelihood is \[
p(y_i|x_i,\beta_0,\beta_1\sigma^2)\propto\exp\left(
-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}(y_i-\beta_0-\beta_1x_i)^2\right)
\] where \(y_i\) is the log(count) for observation \(i\) and \(x_i\) is the log(weight) for observation \(i\). Note that here we think of \(\boldsymbol{y}\) as being random and \(\boldsymbol{x}\) as being fixed. We could, in theory, think of the vector \(\boldsymbol{x}\) as also being random and put a prior on it. But we won’t do that here.
Now, let’s just put uniform priors on \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\) so the priors are proportional to 1. Also, let’s assume \(\sigma^2=1\). This seems reasonable since \(s_e^2\), the MSE, is 0.877. Of course, we could put a prior on \(\sigma^2\) as well and sample it too, but we will focus on only sampling \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\).
Now, with those uniform priors, and plugging in 1 for \(\sigma^2\), we have that the joint posterior of \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\) is: \[
p(\beta_0,\beta_1|\boldsymbol{x},\boldsymbol{y}) \propto\exp\left(
-\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^n(y_i-\beta_0-\beta_1x_i)^2\right)=f(\beta_0,\beta_1|\boldsymbol{x},\boldsymbol{y}).
\]
Then, we can take the log to get \[
\ln(f(\beta_0,\beta_1|\boldsymbol{x},\boldsymbol{y}))=-\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^n(y_i-\beta_0-\beta_1x_i)^2.
\]
Our goal now is to obtain samples of \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\). Let’s use the Metropolis algorithm to do this. Using the log of the function proportional to the joint posterior of \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\), \(\ln\Big(f(\beta_0,\beta_1|\boldsymbol{x},\boldsymbol{y})\Big)\), we’ll write a Metropolis algorithm in R. For \(\beta_0\), we will use a normal proposal distribution centered at the previous value, \(\beta_0^{(i)}\), with a standard deviation of 0.8 and for \(\beta_1\), and we will use a normal proposal distribution centered at the previous value, \(\beta_1^{(i)}\), with a standard deviation of 0.1. The starting values don’t matter too much, but we’ll use \(\beta_0^{(0)}=10\) and \(\beta_1^{(0)}=-0.5\).
This code will generate 10,000 samples and plot the chains for \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\). In addition, the 95% credible interval for \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\) are given.

library(patchwork)
set.seed(2026)
nsamps <- 10000
beta0 <- rep(0, nsamps)
beta1 <- rep(0, nsamps)
beta0[1] <- 10
beta1[1] <- -0.5
for (i in 1:(nsamps - 1)) {
  beta0_star <- rnorm(1, beta0[i], 0.8) # Proposed new value for intercept
  beta1_star <- rnorm(1, beta1[i], 0.1) # Proposed new value for slope
  # y is tree$lcount and x is tree$lweight
  lf1 <- -1 / 2 * sum((tree$lcount - beta0_star - beta1_star * tree$lweight)^2)
  lf2 <- -1 / 2 * sum((tree$lcount - beta0[i] - beta1[i] * tree$lweight)^2)
  if (log(runif(1)) < lf1 - lf2) {
    # Accept the proposed values as new sample values if the above 
    #   condition is satisfied
    beta0[i + 1] <- beta0_star
    beta1[i + 1] <- beta1_star
  } else {
    # Otherwise set the new sample values to be equal to the previous ones
    beta0[i + 1] <- beta0[i]
    beta1[i + 1] <- beta1[i]
  }
}

mcmc_df <- data.frame(index = 1:nsamps, beta0 = beta0, beta1 = beta1)
mcmc_plot1 <- 
  ggplot(mcmc_df, aes(x = index, y = beta0)) +
  geom_line() +
  theme_test() +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 10000, by = 2000)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(8, 11, by = 0.5)) + 
  labs(x = "Index", y = expression(beta[0]))

mcmc_plot2 <- 
  ggplot(mcmc_df, aes(x = index, y = beta1)) +
  geom_line() +
  theme_test() +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 10000, by = 2000)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(-0.75, -0.25, by = 0.1)) + 
  labs(x = "Index", y = expression(beta[1]))

mcmc_plot1 / mcmc_plot2







quantile(beta0, c(.025, .975))

     2.5%     97.5% 
 8.526416 10.210557 

quantile(beta1, c(.025, .975))

      2.5%      97.5% 
-0.6606829 -0.3681291 


The plots of the chains are given in the output above.
The 95% credible interval for \(\beta_0\) is \((8.53, 10.21)\).
The 95% credible interval for \(\beta_1\) is \((-0.661, -0.368)\).
Your intervals may differ a bit due to the random sampling.
Okay, here is the question for you! Based on the plots of the chains above, does it look like the Metropolis sampling worked fairly well? Explain.
The plots show no clear patterns or trends. Both \(\beta_1\) and \(\beta_2\) are centered around a constant level. The chains fluctuate up and down frequently and don’t get stuck at a certain value. So, it looks like the Metropolis sampling worked fairly well.


Part c (3 points)
Interpret both of the credible intervals from part (b).
There is a 95% probability that \(\beta_0\) lies between \((8.53, 10.21)\) and a 95% probability that \(\beta_1\) lies between \((-0.661, -0.368)\).


Part d (5 points)
Find and report the integrated autocorrelation time for the \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\) chains. Each chain will have their own \(\hat{\tau}_{int}\) value, so you should report two (although they will be similar).

tau_beta0=1+2*sum(abs(acf(beta0, lag.max=100, plot=FALSE)$acf))
tau_beta1=1+2*sum(abs(acf(beta1, lag.max=100, plot=FALSE)$acf))
tau_beta0

[1] 25.73468

tau_beta1

[1] 26.61679




Part e (3 points)
Based on the integrated autocorrelation time for the \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\) chains, how many MCMC samples would you need to generate to get the equivalent of 10,000 independent samples?
tau_beta \(\approx 26\)
\(K_{ESS}\approx 10000/26\aprox 385\)
So, \(10000\cdot 26=260,000\) samples are needed


Part f (3 points)
Let’s compare these credible intervals to some other intervals. First, obtain the 95% \(t\) confidence intervals for \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\) just using the confint() function on the model you fit in part (a) and report them here.

confint(treemod)

                 2.5 %     97.5 %
(Intercept)  8.5410148 10.2170375
lweight     -0.6665051 -0.3633051




Part g (8 points)
Now let’s obtain confidence intervals using bootstrapping the cases like we did with in Notes 7 and HW 5. You can use the bootstrapping() function in the math3190package for this or do it yourself with a loop. Set a seed and then using at least 10,000 bootstrap samples, report the 95% percentile confidence intervals for \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\).

set.seed(123)
beta0=numeric(10000)
beta1=numeric(10000)
for(i in 1:10000){
  sample=sample(1:nrow(tree), size=nrow(tree), replace=TRUE)
  boot=tree[sample, ]
  mod=lm(lcount~lweight, boot)
  beta0[i]=coef(mod)[1]
  beta1[i]=coef(mod)[2]}
quantile(beta0, c(0.025,0.975))

     2.5%     97.5% 
 8.390532 10.278994 

quantile(beta1, c(0.025,0.975))

      2.5%      97.5% 
-0.6639041 -0.3509421 




Part i (5 points)
Write a couple sentences comparing all of the intervals in parts (b), (f), and (g).
All three intervals are very similar. This shows that the assumptions for each method were met and implies that the sampling distribution for \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\) are approximately normal. So, the results were consistent across each method.

title: MATH 3190 Homework 7
text:
Note that your homework should be completed in Quarto. You can just add your answers and code to this document in the appropriate part. The file should be rendered as an html document or pdf document (html is quicker to render and you will need an installation of LaTeX to render as a pdf). You will “turn in” this homework by uploading the files, the .qmd and output file, to your GitHub Math_3190_Assignment repository in the Homework/Homework_7 directory. Make sure all supporting files (if there are any) are in this directory as well. You should use git and Unix commands in a terminal for this (i.e. don’t manually upload files to GitHub). In addition, you will submit just your html or pdf file on Canvas to unlock the solutions.
Don’t forget to also put a self-critique comment on Canvas indicating what you did correctly and incorrectly and include any questions you may have about the assignment after submitting the assignment and thoroughly looking through the solutions.

Problem 1 (57 points)
In Homework 6, we briefly looked at a small sample from this data set. Now we have the full data set with 20,640 districts. For districts in California from 1990, we want to predict the median house value for the district based on the district location (longitude and latitude), the median house age in the block group, the total number of rooms in the homes, the total number of bedrooms, the population in the district, the number of households, and the median income (in $10,000). These data were obtained from scikit-learn.org and can be found in the Data folder of the Math3190_Sp26 GitHub repo with the name cal_housing.data.

Part a (5 points)
Read in the data. The read.csv() function works for files that end in .data if they are comma separated like this file is. However, be aware that the housing data does not have column names, so make sure to put header = FALSE. Assign appropriate column names to the columns and then take logs (base e) of all variables except lat and long. Once this is done, split the data into training and testing sets with the testing containing 20% of the values. Set a seed when you do this.

suppressPackageStartupMessages({
library(dplyr)})
housing=read.csv("/Users/siennahardyman/Downloads/cal_housing.data", header=FALSE)
colnames(housing)=c("longitude","latitude","age","rooms","bedrooms","population","occupants","income","value")
housing=housing |> mutate(age=log(age), rooms=log(rooms), bedrooms=log(bedrooms), population=log(population), occupants=log(occupants), income=log(income), value=log(value))


set.seed(1)
test_index=sample(1:nrow(housing), size=round(nrow(housing)*0.2))
housetrain=housing[-test_index,]
housetest=housing[test_index,]



Part b (5 points)
Use the following command to view pair-wise scatter plots for the data here. Change housing_train to whatever you called the training set and be sure to change the eval option for the code chunk to true. Note, that lower option changes the points to be plotted with periods instead of circles. This speeds up plotting time considerably and makes the plots more readable since there are many data points here.
Based on these plots, does multicollinearity appear to be an issue here? Explain.

suppressPackageStartupMessages({
library(GGally)})
ggpairs(housetrain, 
        lower = list(continuous = wrap("points", shape = "."))
)








The plot shows that rooms, bedrooms, and population are highly correlated as well as occupants and value so multicollinearty will be an issue.


Part c (4 points)
Fit a OLS regression model predicting house prices from all other variables. Check its summary output and its VIF values. Comment on the VIFs.

suppressPackageStartupMessages({
library(car)})
house_mod=lm(value~income+occupants+population+bedrooms+rooms+age+latitude+longitude, data=housetrain)
summary(house_mod)
